groups.title: Dolphin Intelligence
groups.desc:
Questions 31-40
Complete the notes below.
Write ONE WORD ONLY for each answer.
groups.collect: Point 1: Not highly intelligent Research on the size of dolphin brains • Dolphins cannot find a way of escaping barriers such as fishing $31$ for catching tuna. • People wrongly thought dolphins to be smart and happy as they often have a $32$. • Small-brained animals such as pigeons and $33$ can perform various complex tasks. - Pigeons are $34$ of themselves. - Pigeons are trained to conditional responses and $35$ reactions. Counter argument • Fatty cells in dolphin brains are linked to protective function and problem-solving skills. Point 2: highly intelligent Research on dolphin behaviour • Billy can imitate the keepers' $36$ through other dolphins. • Karen hides a $37$ for more food. • For the past century, dolphins have been seen as $38$ animals. • Each dolphin produces a unique sound, like the $39$. • They touch each other to make up after a $40$.

questions.title: Dolphins cannot find a way of escaping barriers such as fishing 【   】 for catching tuna.
People wrongly thought dolphins to be smart and happy as they often have a 【   】.
Small-brained animals such as pigeons and 【   】 can perform various complex tasks.
Pigeons are trained to conditional responses and 【   】 reactions.
Billy can imitate the keepers' 【   】 through other dolphins.
Karen hides a 【   】 for more food.
For the past century, dolphins have been seen as 【   】 animals.
Each dolphin produces a unique sound, like the 【   】.
They touch each other to make up after a 【   】.

transcript.en: In today's lecture, we'll dive into the topic of dolphin intelligence, discussing dolphin behaviour and their physical structure in an attempt to shed light on the question: how intelligent are dolphins?
Well, concerning body size, dolphin brains are among the largest in the animal kingdom—more extensive than chimpanzees.
And certain species, like bottlenose dolphins have brains even bigger than humans.
However, some current tests suggest that they do not possess the same cognitive abilities as humans despite having a similar brain size.
For instance, dolphins are often trapped in gill nets originally intended for tuna fishing.
These entanglement cases have led to unnecessary injury or deaths of dolphins, gradually depleting their numbers.
Clearly, dolphins don't realize that they could just jump over these vertical barriers and swim to safety.
People often mistakenly view dolphin's eternal smile as signs of intelligence and joy.
Actually, this is not a facial expression at all, but a fixed design of their head.
Unlike human beings with greater than 40 facial muscles, dolphins have no such powers with which to form an expression.
On the other hand, small-brained animals are sometimes underrated for their level of intelligence.
Pigeons and rats are typical examples of small brain animals which can perform tasks so complex that most humans would have trouble with them.
Over the last few decades, scientists have conducted countless studies on the cognitive abilities of these overlooked animals, with quite surprising results.
Pigeons are aware of their bodies when they see their reflections in the mirror.
Pigeons are also trained under specific conditions and gradually form instinctive reactions.
In an experiment in 2009, a group of 12 pigeons was given a reward like food and water every time they pecked a key on a lab table.
All birds successfully performed the trick.
These test results indicate that brain size is probably not the sole factor in determining intelligence.
Another argument steers the attention to the correlation between intelligence and the constituents of brains rather than their sheer sizes.

It's been found that dolphin brains are comprised chiefly of fatty cells, which serve as a protective barrier between the systemic blood and the extracellular environment of the central nervous system.
They are also essential components of nerve cell membranes, affecting problem-solving skills performance.
Now the second mainstream view is that dolphins are highly intelligent.
Research into the behaviour of dolphins in the wild and in captivity has yielded incredible data on the intelligence of these marine mammals.
Studies show that dolphins not only have the ability to learn as individuals, but those individuals can then pass their new knowledge on to others.
Let's take a look at some typical examples.
Among these are tested with Billy, a dolphin trapped in a sea loch and rescued.
Billy spent three weeks in rehabilitation and was released back into the wild.
There, researchers noticed that Billy had started tail walking, a skill only mastered by captive dolphins who imitated the keepers.
Billy had not been trained to tail-walk but had learned the skill simply by observing other dolphins in the rehab centre.
Another study involves a female dolphin named Karen.
Karen was given a test in which she was rewarded with a fish for every piece of litter she brought to researchers.
To maximise its bounty, she quickly learned to take a newspaper, keep it at the bottom of a tank, and tear off smaller pieces to get more fish.
Over the past hundred years, researchers have proven that dolphins are extremely social species and have evolved to have highly-developed brains.
These factors are the most significant contributors to their intelligence and become the means of survival for dolphins.
In 2008, researchers tracked a group of around 400 dolphins.
One female dolphin in the group was having a lot of trouble swimming and kept flipping upside down or sinking into the water.
The other dolphins crowded around it.
They paddled side by side with the injured female on their backs.
By keeping the wounded female above water, they may have helped it to breathe, avoiding drowning.
So how do they communicate with each other?
Well, scientists have discovered through observation and meticulous testing that every dolphin has a different sound resembling the whistle that other dolphins recognize as a particular individual.
Dolphins can emit a wide variety of sounds.
The frequency levels range ten times beyond what humans can hear.
But of course, there are other means of communication for dolphins besides sound.
During mating season, male dolphins stroke females with flippers after a fight to affirm social bonds.

31.analysis:
听前预测：定位词为 brains、fishing、tuna，提示词为 cannot、such as，此处应填名词，表示某种障碍物。
题目解析：副标题中的 brains 一词先出现，但其与本题相关信息间隔较远，考生需耐心往下听。先听到题干的 cannot ... escaping 被替换为 trapped in，接下来听到 nets 一词，符合题干要求，先填写下来。随后听到定位词 tuna、fishing，可以进一步确认答案。本题较难，一是定位信息出现得较晚，二是 gill nets 作为一种比较专业的渔网，加大了考生提取答案词的难度。注意，听录音时要带着目的去听，听不懂的地方不一定影响做题，关键是要继续往下听。

32.analysis:
听前预测：定位词为 happy，提示词为 a，此处应填以辅音字母开头的单数名词，并且与某种观点相关。
题目解析：先听到题干的 wrongly thought 被替换为 mistakenly view，随后听到符合要求的名词 smile，先填写下来。继续往下听，听到题干的 smart and happy 被替换为 intelligence and joy，可以进一步确认答案。

33.analysis:
听前预测：定位词为 small-brained animals、pigeons，提示词为 such as、and，此处应填名词，表示某种脑容量小的动物。
题目解析：定位词原词重现，随后听到 rats 一词，符合题干要求，填写下来即可。

34.analysis:
听前预测：定位词为 pigeons；提示词为 are，此处应填形容词或过去分词，注意听描述鸽子的信息。
题目解析：定位词原词重现，随后听到 aware of their bodies，其中 their bodies 对应题干的 themselves，可以判断 aware 为正确答案。

35.analysis:
听前预测：定位词为 conditional response，提示词为 reactions，此处应填名词或形容词。
题目解析：本题与上一题联系较为紧密，可通过逻辑信号词 also 辅助定位。先听到定位词被替换为 specific conditions，接着听到 instinctive reactions，将 instinctive 填写下来即可。本题涉及的词汇有一定难度，考生平时要多积累与动物有关的词汇。

36.analysis:
听前预测：定位词为 Billy，提示词为 imitate the keepers'，此处应填名词，表示某种行为举止。
题目解析：定位词原词重现，随后需注意听描述行为举止类的信息。先听到 tail walking，属于举止类信息，但题目要求的是人类行为且只能填写一个单词，因此 walking 为正确答案。随后听到的一系列信息，比如 imitated 等，均可进一步确认答案。本题难度较大，题干结构和原文的对应度较低，考生要带着目的去听信息，而不是单纯地翻译句子。

37.analysis:
听前预测：定位词为 Karen、food，提示词为 hides、a，此处应填以辅音字母开头的单数名词，表示某种物品。
题目解析：定位词 Karen 先出现，随后听到 litter 一词，该词为不可数名词，不符合题干的语法要求，可以排除。继续往下听，听到 newspaper 一词，符合题干要求，先填写下来。随后听到题干的 more food 被替换为 more fish，可以进一步确认答案。若做题时不确定 litter 是否符合要求，可以先记录下来，等听到更合适的信息时再修正。注意，newspaper 既可以用作可数名词，又可以用作不可数名词。

38.analysis:
听前预测：定位词为 the past century，提示词为 animals，此处应填形容词或名词。
题目解析：先听到定位词被替换为 the past hundred years，随后听到 social species，其中 species 对应题干的 animals，因此 social 为正确答案。

39.analysis:
听前预测：定位词为 unique sound，提示词为 like，此处应填名词，表示某种声音。
题目解析：定位词被替换为 different sound，并且和上一题间隔较远，考生注意通过设问句来判断题目的转换。定位信息出现后，听到题干的 like 被替换为 resemble，可以判断与之搭配的 whistle 为正确答案。

40.analysis:
听前预测：提示词为 a，此处应填以辅音字母开头的单数名词。
题目解析：本题可以通过逻辑信号词 other 来判断题目的转换。先听到题干的 touch 被替换为 stroke，随后听到 after a fight，可以判断 fight 为正确答案。虽然题干 touch 的替换词 stroke 较难理解，但其并不是解题的必要条件，关键在于定位和抓取信息。
